content: An electron is moving in a circle of radius 2m with speed of 4 m/s. Find the acceleration of the electron.
explanation:
Given, radius of circular path, $$r=2 \mathrm{~m}$$

Speed of circular path, $$v=4 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$$

Acceleration of electron moving on circular path with constant speed is equal to centripetal acceleration.

$$\therefore$$ Acceleration of electron $$(a)=$$ Centripetal acceleration

$$a=\frac{v^2}{r}=\frac{4^2}{2}=8 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$$

content: The particle of mass $$m$$ is moving in a circular path of constant radius $$r$$ such that its centripetal acceleration $$a_c$$ is varying with time $$t$$ as $$a_c=k^2 r t^2$$, where $$k$$ is a constant. The power delivered to particle by the forces acting on it is
explanation:
If v is instantaneous velocity, centripetal acceleration

$$\begin{aligned} a_c & =\frac{v^2}{r} \\ \therefore \quad \frac{v^2}{r} & =k^2 r t^2 \\ \Rightarrow \quad v & =k r t \quad\left[\because a_c=k^2 r t^2 \text { is given }\right] \end{aligned}$$

In circular motion, work done by centripetal force is always zero and work is done only by tangential force.

$$\therefore$$ Tangential acceleration

$$a_t=\frac{d v}{d t}=\frac{d}{d t}(k r t)=k r$$

$$\therefore$$ Tangential force $$F_t=m a_t=m k r$$

Power $$P=F_t v=(m k r)(k r t)=m k^2 r^2 t$$

content:
Assertion : For looping a vertical loop of radius, $$r$$ the minimum velocity at lowest point should be $$\sqrt{5 g r}$$.

Reason : In this event the velocity at the highest point will be zero.

options.content: Both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion
Both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion
Assertion is true but reason is false
Both assertion and reason are false.

explanation:
At the lowest point of a vertical circle, the minimum velocity at the bottom $$u_{\min }=\sqrt{5 g r}$$

At $$u=\sqrt{5 g r}$$

Velocity at highest point $$v=\sqrt{g r}$$
